stem:
What will this Python code print?

print('Hello, World!')
explanation_after: print() is Python's way of outputting text. The text inside the quotes is displayed exactly as written. This is traditionally the first program every programmer writes.

stem: Which of these is valid Python code?
explanation_after: Python is case-sensitive (print not PRINT). Strings must be in quotes. print(hello) would look for a variable called hello, not the text 'hello'.

stem: Why is Python particularly popular for AI and machine learning?

options.text: It was invented specifically for AI
It has a vast ecosystem of ML libraries and readable syntax
AI can only be coded in Python

explanation_after: Python's readable syntax lowers the barrier to entry. Its ecosystem — NumPy, PyTorch, TensorFlow, scikit-learn, Hugging Face — is unmatched. Speed-critical parts are written in C/C++ underneath.

prompt:
Complete this Python code to print your name:

print(________)
explanation_after: In Python, text (strings) must be wrapped in quotes — either single 'like this' or double "like this". Without quotes, Python treats it as a variable name.
